llm.prompt: We called the dot product a similarity meter, and the breadcrumb question was: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size? I will tell you the fix I came up with; your job is to stress-test it. Show me a case where the raw dot product and my proposed fix would rank two candidate vectors in a different order, and check whether my fix really keeps only the direction. Use the aligned / perpendicular / opposed sign behavior from the sweep to check your example.
llm.whatGoodLooksLike: A good answer produces two vectors where a long-but-misaligned vector beats a short-but-aligned one on raw dot product, then flips the ranking once the learner's fix removes the length, and explains the flip using length times projection rather than just asserting it.

llm.prompt: The meter shows up in transformers as scaled dot-product attention, next session's machine if you want a preview tonight: query dot key, divided by sqrt(d_k), fed into a softmax. Explain to me why the divide-by-sqrt(d_k) step exists, what breaks in the softmax if you skip it, and how that connects back to the meter reading you get from turning the probe in the geometric sweep.
llm.whatGoodLooksLike: A good answer talks about dot products growing with dimension so the pre-softmax scores get too large and saturate the softmax into near one-hot outputs, and ties that growth back to the length-times-projection picture rather than treating sqrt(d_k) as a magic constant.

llm.prompt: I just named the machine I've been using all along: the dot product. Give me three places outside language models where the same meter shows up under a different name in the same aisle, like we did in "same meter, different aisle." For each one tell me what gets treated as query and what gets treated as key, and what a high score means in that domain.
llm.whatGoodLooksLike: A good answer names concrete domains (recommendation matching, document search, image embedding lookup, and similar) and for each one correctly identifies the two vectors being compared and states what a high score physically means there, not just "similarity."

papers.title: Attention Is All You Need
papers.hook: Read the attention equation once and you will see tonight's meter in its work uniform: a dot product, divided by sqrt(d_k), then softmaxed, scoring every word against every other word.

papers.title: Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate
papers.hook: Before the dot product got the job, alignment scoring was done a different way. Reading this next to tonight's session shows you that the similarity meter is the idea; the dot product is just the cheapest gauge anyone found for it.

papers.title: Dot products and duality
papers.hook: This is the geometric grammar underneath the sweep you did tonight: projection times length, with the same aligned-positive, perpendicular-zero, opposed-negative sign behavior, drawn out instead of described.

projects.title: Build the honest-look ranking flip
projects.brief: In NumPy, make five short "document" vectors and one "query" vector, all with different lengths on purpose. Rank the documents two ways: by raw dot product with the query, and by the cheapest fix you proposed for the breadcrumb. Print both rankings side by side. Finish line: at least one document changes rank between the two lists, and you can point to its length as the reason, proving your proposed fix really does forget the size and keep only the direction.

projects.title: Turn the probe into a tiny attention layer
projects.brief: Using only NumPy, encode four short toy "words" as small random vectors, pick one as the query, and compute scaled dot-product attention (query dot each key, divide by sqrt(d_k), softmax) over the other three as keys and values. Finish line: print the attention weights and confirm the word whose vector you deliberately made most aligned with the query gets the highest weight, so you can watch the similarity meter drive the machine you named tonight.
